title: James Henley Thornwell
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 10-22-23 - James Henley Thornwell - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Two Controversies that Shaped American Presbyterianism
subpoints: Old Side / New Side controversy (1706–1758): dispute over the role of the Westminster Standards in ordination vs. prioritizing subjective conversion experience; Gilbert Tennent's "The Danger of an Unconverted Ministry" (1740) caused the split; reunion in 1758 was dominated by New Side men

Old School / New School controversy (1830s–1857): second great awakening revivalism vs. ordinary means of grace; Charles Finney as representative of new school methods; the anxious bench and altar call arose from new school; church split into four in 1857 and Civil War
All five men in this series — Alexander, Miller, Hodge, Thornwell, Dabney — were old side / old school men; the PCA traces its heritage to this tradition

outline.title: The Life of James Henley Thornwell
subpoints: Born December 9, 1812, in South Carolina; father died before his eighth birthday; mother Martha Terrell Thornwell raised him in faith — the role of godly mothers is a recurring theme across all five biographies
Brilliant scholar: entered College of South Carolina at nearly 16 as a junior after intensive study; graduated as valedictorian and delivered his speech in Latin

Spiritually adrift during college — confronted by Socinianism and deism; found the Westminster Confession for 25 cents in a Columbia bookstore and read it through the night: "I felt that I had met with a system which held together with the strictest logical connection"
Professed faith in Christ; became a Presbyterian; studied at Andover Seminary briefly (found it full of New School men) and Harvard; became professor at the University of South Carolina

Married Nancy White Witherspoon, great-great-niece of John Witherspoon — the only minister to sign the Declaration of Independence; died at age 49, four months before his 50th birthday, of tuberculosis

outline.title: Controversy 1: Jure Divino — What Did God Prescribe for Church Government?
subpoints: Both Hodge and Thornwell agreed that God appointed Presbyterian church government; they disagreed on how specifically God prescribed it
Hodge: God gave principles of church government; the church figures out the particulars (e.g., who qualifies as an elder); this led the northern church to a two-office denomination (elder and deacon)

Thornwell: God prescribed not only the what but the how — the Bible teaches the offices specifically; this led the southern church and the OPC to a three-office denomination (minister, ruling elder, deacon)
The PCA follows Hodge's two-office model; Covenant OPC follows Thornwell's three-office model; this debate is still alive in Reformed and Presbyterian churches today

outline.title: Controversy 2: Chattel Slavery
subpoints: Thornwell argued that slavery as practiced in the South was consistent with biblical Christianity — a position Pastor Tito explicitly condemns as inexcusable and wrong

Thornwell's error: he constructed an idealized, romantic version of the Christian plantation that papered over the brutal reality of antebellum slavery; he confused what the Bible addresses (regulated, non-chattel servitude) with the chattel slavery actually practiced

Thornwell preached that it was the Christian master's duty to educate slaves and bring them to church, and he preached against cruelty — but these became peripheral arguments rather than challenges to the institution

His famous 1850 sermon "The Rights and Duties of Masters" framed the conflict as Christianity vs. atheism, with the South defending Christian civilization; Lincoln's Republican Party victory led him to support South Carolina's secession

Historical charity vs. historical dishonesty: we judge people by the times they lived in, learn from their errors, and make sure we do not repeat them; we also ask what sins we may be blind to in our own time

summary:
In this Sunday School lesson on James Henley Thornwell, Pastor Tito Lyro situates Thornwell within the broader controversies that shaped American Presbyterianism. The lesson opens with a detailed overview of two defining Presbyterian controversies: the Old Side / New Side split (1706–1758) over confessional subscription and revivalism, and the Old School / New School controversy (1830s–1857) over ordinary means of grace versus the anxious bench and decision theology. All five men in the biographical series were old school confessionalists, and the PCA traces its heritage to this tradition.

Thornwell's life is traced from his origins in South Carolina — where his father died before his eighth birthday, leaving his godly mother to raise him — through his brilliant academic career (valedictorian at the University of South Carolina, speech delivered in Latin) and his unexpected conversion through reading the Westminster Confession of Faith, which he purchased for 25 cents and read through the night. The lesson covers his two major theological controversies: his debate with Charles Hodge over the *jure divino* basis for church government (which still divides Presbyterian denominations into two-office and three-office bodies today), and his defense of chattel slavery, which Pastor Tito straightforwardly calls inexcusable and wrong. Thornwell constructed an idealized version of the Christian plantation that failed to reckon with the brutality of antebellum slavery as it was actually practiced. The lesson closes with a call to historical charity without historical dishonesty — we learn from the past without whitewashing it, and we soberly ask what sins of our own time we may be blind to.
transcript:
## Introduction: Two Controversies

For us, we're going to continue our Reformation series for this month as we look at American Presbyterians who were very significant for how we think today in the 21st century. Our topic for today is James Henley Thornwell. We want to become familiar with him.

One difficulty in studying 19th century theologians — the leading theologians and pastors, especially from the American South — is their defense of what is called chattel slavery as it was practiced at the time. They seem to unanimously defend the kind of slavery practiced in the time. So it becomes difficult to listen to them in other areas where they may be helpful because there is a glaring deficiency on their part. We will try our best to learn something good from Thornwell, especially because the slavery he actually defended was not really the slavery that was being practiced in the South — even though he claimed it was. He created a very delicate notion of slavery and then tried to say this was a good thing. In no way do we condone slavery here of any sort.

### The Five Men in Chronological Order

In God's providence — and not because of any extraordinary planning abilities of our own — we are addressing these five American leaders in chronological order, which actually makes it helpful to understand the development of thought in the United States as we see these different controversies. Archibald Alexander was covered by Andrew Hoye. Then Samuel Miller. Then Charles Hodge — that was last week. Today, James Henley Thornwell. And next week, Elder Nick Anderson will cover Robert Dabney.

### The Old Side / New Side Controversy

Before we get to Thornwell himself, I want to briefly touch on two big controversies that formed these men, because several of them have been mentioned but we have not yet defined them clearly.

In 1706, American Presbyterianism began when the first presbytery was established in Philadelphia. A big name associated with that is Francis McKemie. By 1717, the first synod was established — the Synod of Philadelphia, made of three presbyteries. By the 1720s, there was a great dispute about the role of the Westminster Standards in the ordination of ministers.

The Old Side insisted that subscribing to the Standards was necessary in order to be ordained as a minister. The New Side said that the Bible should be the standard — which everyone agreed, but they used this as a cover for something else. They also said that more important than what the man believed was this: did he have a conversion experience? A dateable moment of conversion? A dramatic personal experience? Those subjective personal experiences were more important, they said, than what the man believed.

The Old Side said: your experience is going to be a reflection of your belief. What you believe is very important. An unsatisfying compromise was reached in 1729 when the Synod passed an act adopting the Westminster Standards as the official confession of the church. But the promise was that you only had to subscribe to what were considered the "essential parts" — and they never listed what those were. So the Old Side got the confession formally adopted, but in practice, pastors on the New Side could define the essentials as they pleased.

The controversy died in the 1740s when Gilbert Tennent — a Presbyterian minister in New Jersey — preached his famous sermon "The Danger of an Unconverted Ministry" in 1740. His argument essentially was: whoever does not agree with me about the Great Awakening is not saved. And then he told people: if your pastor disagrees with the Great Awakening, leave your church. He lambasted the cautious Old Side preachers, calling them "as blind as moles and as dead as stones without any spiritual taste and relish." That is an *ad hominem* attack rather than dealing with the actual argument.

This sermon caused a split in 1742 that lasted until 1758, when the two groups reunited. But the new united Synod was dominated by New Side men — 72 to 22. The church became a New Side church, with itinerant preaching and a more relaxed view of confessional subscription becoming standard.

All the men in this series are Old Side men. They would have fit that mold of demanding allegiance to the Standards — not just a general subscription to the "system of doctrine," but careful confessional integrity. And this fight is still being fought today in every Presbyterian body over how much of the confession and catechisms a man must believe in order to be ordained.

### The Old School / New School Controversy

About a hundred years later — because Presbyterians like fighting — there was another controversy. Again, not very creatively named: Old School and New School. In the 1820s, 30s, 40s, and 60s, there was the Second Great Awakening, and revival again. In essence you had one side saying, let the Bible dictate what we do, and the other side saying, whatever works, whatever fills the church, whatever creates the most emotional attachment — that is what you follow.

The Old School Presbyterians, led by Charles Hodge in the north and eventually by Thornwell in the south, were much more conservative and not supportive of unbridled revivalism. The big name of the Second Great Awakening is Charles Finney. An interesting contrast: there is no church established during the Finney revivals that still believes the Bible today. But there are quite a few churches in which Asahel Nettleton worked — a good evangelist who worked alongside local pastors — that are still faithful, Bible-believing churches to this day.

The New School Presbyterians were more related to New England. They believed that the old Calvinism kept getting in the way of actual conversions. Several things developed out of the New School: the anxious bench — where those who were convicted would come to the front pew so the preacher could preach to them more intensely until they made a decision for Christ — and then from the anxious bench came the altar call. The Old School was saying: it is the Spirit of God that saves through the preaching of the Word; it does not matter where you are sitting. God works through the ordinary means of preaching, the sacraments, prayer, and fellowship. We do not need these extra parachurch events to revive the church.

In 1857 the church split into two — and then into four during the Civil War, dividing along both theological and geographical lines. The southern church became the Presbyterian Church in the United States. The northern church eventually became the Presbyterian Church in the United States of America (PCUSA). These two joined in 1983, forming the current PCUSA — which is a very liberal church today, no longer affirming the Bible as God's word or Jesus as God incarnate. The PCA was formed in the early 1970s when conservatives in the Southern Church broke away rather than merge with the Northern Church. Covenant College and Covenant Seminary — the primary college and seminary of the PCA — were founded by men who left the Bible Presbyterian Church in 1956 and eventually merged with the PCA in 1982.

## The Life of James Henley Thornwell

With that background, let us come to Thornwell. His biographers call him "a Prince of Israel" — and they say there were two such princes: Charles Hodge and James Henley Thornwell. They were contemporaries who interacted often. Even though they agreed 99% of the time, that 1% they disagreed about they fought about a great deal.

One of the things I found interesting about these five men is the role that their mothers played in their upbringing. In God's providence, it seems that more hearts have been won for Christ on the knees of mothers than through any big name in history. Thornwell's father died when he was very young, and his mother was the one who raised him in the Lord — having to figure out how to feed the children, even renting out rooms in the house to make ends meet (just as Mrs. Hodge had done after her husband died).

### Early Life

Thornwell was born in December 1812 in South Carolina — he was the South Carolinian of all South Carolinians. He showed great aptitude for learning early in life; two able men — a farmer and a rising lawyer — sponsored his education. He could not go to school early enough because there was not enough money, so he went to college late — just before he turned 16.

He enrolled at the College of South Carolina (today the University of South Carolina — what people in the Southeast call the original USC, being older than the University of Southern California). He took a placement test hoping to enter as a junior, failed, and was accepted as a sophomore. But he spent two weeks studying intensively, retook the test, and was accepted as a junior. He graduated in less than two years as valedictorian, delivering his valedictorian address in Latin.

He planned to be a lawyer to honor the man who had paid for his education. But he struggled spiritually while in college — confronted with Socinianism (a form of anti-trinitarianism, the closest parallel today might be Unitarianism) and with deism (the view that God created everything but then stepped back from the world). He could see that both positions were untenable but had no theological framework to counter them.

One day, as his biographer B.M. Palmer records, while strolling in Columbia, South Carolina, Thornwell stumbled into a bookstore and found on the counter a small volume entitled *Confession of Faith*. He had never before heard of its existence. He only saw that it contained a systematic exposition of Christian doctrine. It was the Westminster Confession. He bought it for 25 cents — the biographer includes that detail; perhaps the price surprised him too — carried it home, and as he himself testified, read it entirely through the night. He says: "For the first time I felt that I had met with a system which held together with the strictest logical connection. Granting its premises, the conclusions were bound to follow." This book determined him as a Calvinist and a Presbyterian — although he had never been in contact with this branch of the church and had been inside a Presbyterian sanctuary only once.

Some months later he professed faith in Christ, became a member of Concord Presbyterian Church in Sumterville, South Carolina, and decided to be a pastor rather than a lawyer.

### Seminary and Early Ministry

To be trained for the ministry he went to Andover Seminary in New England. He arrived and found himself surrounded by New School men. He also had to board with Unitarian students because that was all that was available; he did not get along with them. Because of that and the harsh New England winters, he eventually came back south. He spent a year at Harvard brushing up on his Hebrew, then returned to South Carolina and became a professor at what is today the University of South Carolina. In fact, the main administrative building at the University of South Carolina today is the Thornwell Building — though there are efforts to change the name because of his positions on slavery.

He married Nancy White Witherspoon — a descendant of John Witherspoon, the only minister to sign the Declaration of Independence and also former president of what is today Princeton University. Thornwell died in 1862, four months before his 50th birthday, of tuberculosis.

## Controversy 1: Jure Divino — Church Government

The first major controversy of his life is called *jure divino* — meaning, by divine right. What did God appoint for the government of the church? Thornwell and Hodge agreed that God appointed Presbyterianism for the government of the church. But they disagreed on how specifically God prescribed it.

Hodge thought God gave principles — figure out how it works. So who is an elder? Figure that out from Scripture and from practice. The northern church became a follower of Hodge's position, becoming a two-office denomination: the office of elder (with two functions: teaching and ruling) and the office of deacon. The PCA follows this model.

Thornwell said no — God prescribed not only the *what* but the *how*. The Bible is more specific than Hodge allowed. So the southern church and the OPC became three-office denominations: the minister of the Word, the ruling elder, and the deacon. This debate is still alive and well in every Presbyterian body.

## Controversy 2: Chattel Slavery

The second big controversy is chattel slavery — the slavery practiced here in the United States and throughout the Americas. In chattel slavery, a person is a commodity like an animal. They have no rights, they are not considered fully human, and they are used until they are no longer useful and then discarded.

Thornwell argued that slavery as practiced in the South was consistent with biblical Christianity. I think most Christians today — at least I hope most — would struggle to reconcile Thornwell's learned orthodoxy with this defense of slavery. He claimed there was no explicit condemnation of the slavery practiced in the South in the Bible. But he did not seem to realize that what the Bible addresses under the word "slavery" is a fundamentally different institution from what was practiced in the antebellum South.

In fact, even in the New Testament, Paul says in 1 Corinthians 7 that if you are a slave and are able to be free, do whatever you can to become free. So even in that context where there were regulations governing servitude, the goal was the elimination of the institution. But Thornwell went so far as to argue that the Southern slave system was better than what he called the chaotic northern factory system, where no one cared about the workers. He crafted a romanticized version of the idyllic Christian plantation that papered over the ugly exploitation of the slaves in antebellum slavery.

As the crisis between North and South grew more acute in the 1850s, Southern theologians such as Thornwell became consumed with what they saw as the threat posed by radical northern abolitionists who would pull out the props that undergirded Southern society since the colonial period. His famous sermon "The Rights and Duties of Masters" from the 1850s declared: "The parties in this conflict are not merely abolitionists and slaveholders. They are atheists, socialists, communists, Red Republicans, Jacobins on the one side, and the friends of order and regulated freedom on the other." He declared the South the defenders of Christianity and the North the atheists. When Lincoln — the candidate of the northern Republican Party — was elected president, Thornwell supported South Carolina's secession from the Union.

## Conclusion: Historical Charity Without Historical Dishonesty

As we study history and go through a series like this one, we learn that the people considered giants of the faith are just that — people. People with flaws, shaped by the times they lived in. We learn from history and benefit from what these people did and wrote, but we do that through the lens of Scripture. We also learn to judge them as citizens of their own times, not ours — and that is something our culture has forgotten. We try to judge everyone according to today's prevailing positions instead of judging people according to the times they lived.

Thornwell's position on chattel slavery is inexcusable. He was wrong. His defense of slavery really found its anchor in a kind of theological sand. But he is not unique for the time in which he lived. The study of history requires a certain level of charity — that we judge people according to the times they lived, not according to how we think they should have lived in light of today's beliefs and culture.

And I will say clearly: I am in no way suggesting today that we should ever support slavery or that the slavery practiced in the United States was correct. I have said the opposite from this pulpit. What I am saying is that we judge people according to the times they lived and exercise Christian charity in that judgment.

Let's pray. And thank you for history — for the things we can learn that are good, and the things we can learn not to repeat. We pray that we take those things to heart. For us, pray that in worshiping you this morning, the best of the United States be given to us. We pray in Jesus' name. Amen.
